questions.question: What are the three axioms of functional dependency for the relational databases given by Armstrong?
questions.answer: Armstrong's three axioms are: (1) Reflexivity: If Y is a subset of X, then X -> Y. (2) Augmentation: If X -> Y, then XZ -> YZ for any Z. (3) Transitivity: If X -> Y and Y -> Z, then X -> Z.
questions.to_find: State Armstrong's three axioms of functional dependency.

understand.plain: Armstrong's axioms are a set of three inference rules used to derive all functional dependencies in a relational database schema. They are sound (derive only valid FDs) and complete (derive all valid FDs). Every other derived rule (union, decomposition, pseudo-transitivity) can be proven from these three.

keywords.explain: A constraint between two sets of attributes in a relation: X -> Y means the values of X uniquely determine the values of Y.
keywords.example: In an Employee table, EmployeeID -> Name means each employee ID maps to exactly one name.

keywords.explain: A minimal, sound, and complete set of inference rules for deriving all valid functional dependencies from a given set.
keywords.example: From A -> B and B -> C, by Transitivity we derive A -> C.

keywords.explain: Any set of attributes functionally determines itself or any of its subsets.
keywords.example: If Y subset_of X, then X -> Y. E.g., {A,B} -> {A}.

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 520 220' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='10' y='10' width='500' height='200' rx='10' class='bg'/><text x='260' y='40' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Armstrong's Axioms</text><rect x='30' y='55' width='140' height='55' rx='6' class='hi'/><text x='100' y='75' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>Reflexivity</text><text x='100' y='95' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>Y subset X => X->Y</text><rect x='190' y='55' width='140' height='55' rx='6' class='hi'/><text x='260' y='75' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>Augmentation</text><text x='260' y='95' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>X->Y => XZ->YZ</text><rect x='350' y='55' width='140' height='55' rx='6' class='hi'/><text x='420' y='75' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>Transitivity</text><text x='420' y='95' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>X->Y, Y->Z => X->Z</text><text x='260' y='145' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>These 3 axioms are Sound and Complete</text><text x='260' y='170' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>All other FD rules are derived from these.</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Three boxes showing the three Armstrong axioms: Reflexivity (Y subset X implies X->Y), Augmentation (X->Y implies XZ->YZ), and Transitivity (X->Y and Y->Z implies X->Z).

given.aim: State the three fundamental axioms of functional dependency (Armstrong's axioms).

terms.meaning: If Y is a subset of X, then X -> Y (every set of attributes determines its subsets).
terms.connects: Allows trivial FDs; forms the base of the axiom system.

terms.meaning: If X -> Y holds, then XZ -> YZ for any set of attributes Z.
terms.example: If A -> B, then AC -> BC.
terms.connects: Lets us add the same attributes to both sides of an FD.

terms.meaning: If X -> Y and Y -> Z, then X -> Z.
terms.example: If A -> B and B -> C, then A -> C.
terms.connects: Chains FDs together; key to computing attribute closures.

given.plan: Recall and state all three axioms precisely: Reflexivity, Augmentation, and Transitivity. These together form a sound and complete basis for deriving all functional dependencies.

steps.title: Axiom 1 — Reflexivity
steps.formula_raw: If Y subset_of X, then X -> Y
steps.apply: Any superset of attributes functionally determines any subset of itself. This produces trivial FDs.
steps.note: Called 'trivial' because the determined attributes are already part of the determinant.

steps.title: Axiom 2 — Augmentation
steps.formula_raw: If X -> Y, then XZ -> YZ (for any Z)
steps.apply: Adding the same attribute set Z to both sides of a valid FD preserves validity.
steps.note: Also written as: X -> Y implies XZ -> Y (since YZ -> Y by Reflexivity).

steps.title: Axiom 3 — Transitivity
steps.formula_raw: If X -> Y and Y -> Z, then X -> Z
steps.apply: FDs can be chained. If X determines Y and Y determines Z, then X also determines Z.
steps.note: This is the most commonly used axiom in closure computation and normalization proofs.

solution.result: Armstrong's three axioms: (1) Reflexivity: Y subset X => X -> Y; (2) Augmentation: X -> Y => XZ -> YZ; (3) Transitivity: X -> Y and Y -> Z => X -> Z.

questions.formula_note: Armstrong's Axioms: a sound and complete set of three inference rules (Reflexivity, Augmentation, Transitivity) for functional dependencies in relational databases.

questions.question:
Using Armstrong's axioms of functional dependency, derive the following rule:
{x -> y, y -> z} |= xz -> yz

(Note: x -> y denotes y is functionally dependent on x; z subset_of y denotes z is subset of y; and |= means derives).
questions.answer: Proof: (1) x -> y (given). (2) By Augmentation on x -> y with z: xz -> yz. Hence {x->y, y->z} |= xz -> yz.
questions.to_find: Derive xz -> yz from {x -> y, y -> z} using Armstrong's axioms.

understand.plain: We need to prove that if x determines y, and y determines z, then xz (x combined with z) determines yz (y combined with z). This is done using the Augmentation axiom applied to the first FD.

keywords.explain: If X -> Y then XZ -> YZ for any Z.
A set of FDs F |= f means f can be derived from F using Armstrong's axioms.

understand.visual_alt: From x -> y, augment with z on both sides to get xz -> yz directly by the Augmentation axiom.

given.aim: Derive xz -> yz from {x -> y, y -> z} using Armstrong's axioms.

terms.connects: The FD we apply Augmentation to.
Not directly needed for this derivation but part of the given set.

terms.meaning: If X -> Y, then XZ -> YZ for any Z.
terms.connects: Directly applied to x->y with attribute z to get xz->yz.

given.plan: Apply the Augmentation axiom to x -> y by adding z to both sides, yielding xz -> yz directly.

steps.title: Use given FD x -> y
steps.apply: This is provided as a premise in the set of FDs.
steps.note: We will apply Augmentation to this FD.

steps.title: Apply Augmentation with z
steps.apply: By the Augmentation axiom, adding z to both sides of x -> y gives: xz -> yz.
steps.note: The second FD y -> z is not needed for this particular derivation; xz -> yz follows directly from x -> y alone.

solution.result: xz -> yz is derived from x -> y by Augmentation (adding z to both sides).

questions.formula_note: Augmentation axiom: X -> Y => XZ -> YZ; applied with Z = z to FD x -> y.

questions.question:
Using Armstrong's axioms of functional dependency, derive the following rule:
{x -> y, wy -> z} |= xw -> z

(Note: x -> y denotes y is functionally dependent on x; z subset_of y denotes z is subset of y; and |= means derives).
questions.answer: Proof: (1) x -> y (given). (2) By Augmentation: xw -> yw. (3) wy -> z (given). (4) By Transitivity on (2) and (3): xw -> z.
questions.to_find: Derive xw -> z from {x -> y, wy -> z} using Armstrong's axioms.

understand.plain: We have two FDs: x determines y, and wy together determine z. We need to show that xw (x combined with w) also determines z. The key insight is to augment x->y with w to get xw->yw, and then use transitivity since wy->z.

keywords.explain: If X -> Y then XZ -> YZ for any attribute set Z.
If X -> Y and Y -> Z then X -> Z.
keywords.example: AW -> BW and BW -> C implies AW -> C.

understand.visual_alt: Chain: xw -> yw (by augmenting x->y with w) then xw -> z (by transitivity with wy->z, since yw = wy).

given.aim: Derive xw -> z from {x -> y, wy -> z} using Armstrong's axioms.

terms.meaning: First given FD: x determines y.
Second given FD: the combination of w and y determines z.
terms.connects: Used in the Transitivity step after augmenting x->y.

terms.meaning: Intermediate derived FD obtained by augmenting x->y with w.
terms.connects: Bridges to wy->z via Transitivity (xw->yw and wy->z => xw->z).

given.plan: Step 1: Augment x->y with w to get xw->yw. Step 2: Note yw = wy. Step 3: Apply Transitivity with wy->z to get xw->z.

steps.title: Augment x -> y with w
steps.apply: By Augmentation axiom, adding w to both sides of x -> y gives xw -> yw.
steps.note: Attribute set yw is the same as wy (order of attributes in a set doesn't matter).

steps.title: Apply Transitivity
steps.formula_raw: xw -> yw (= wy) and wy -> z => xw -> z (Transitivity)
steps.apply: We now have xw -> wy (from step 1) and wy -> z (given). By Transitivity, xw -> z.
steps.note: This derived rule is sometimes called Pseudo-transitivity: X->Y and WY->Z implies XW->Z.

solution.result: xw -> z is derived using Augmentation on x->y (to get xw->yw) followed by Transitivity with wy->z.

questions.formula_note: Pseudo-transitivity derived rule: X->Y and WY->Z implies XW->Z; proven via Augmentation then Transitivity from Armstrong's axioms.

questions.question:
Using Armstrong's axioms of functional dependency, derive the following rule:
{x -> y, z subset_of y} |= x -> z

(Note: x -> y denotes y is functionally dependent on x; z subset_of y denotes z is subset of y; and |= means derives).
questions.answer: Proof: (1) z subset_of y (given). (2) By Reflexivity: y -> z. (3) x -> y (given). (4) By Transitivity on (3) and (2): x -> z.
questions.to_find: Derive x -> z from {x -> y, z subset_of y} using Armstrong's axioms.

understand.plain: We know x determines y, and z is a subset of y. We want to show x also determines z. Since z is a subset of y, by Reflexivity y determines z. Then chaining x->y and y->z by Transitivity gives x->z.

keywords.explain: If Z is a subset of Y, then Y -> Z (a set determines any of its subsets).
keywords.example: {A,B} -> {A} because A is a subset of {A,B}.

keywords.explain: If X -> Y and Y -> Z, then X -> Z.
keywords.example: A -> BC and BC -> B implies A -> B.

understand.visual_alt: Since z is a subset of y, Reflexivity gives y->z. Combined with x->y, Transitivity gives x->z. This is the Decomposition derived rule.

given.aim: Derive x -> z from {x -> y, z subset_of y} using Armstrong's axioms.

terms.meaning: First given FD: x determines y.
terms.connects: First FD in the Transitivity chain.

terms.meaning: z is a subset of the attribute set y.
terms.connects: Enables Reflexivity to give y -> z.

terms.meaning: Since z is a subset of y, by Reflexivity y functionally determines z.
terms.connects: Second FD in the Transitivity chain.

given.plan: Step 1: Apply Reflexivity to z subset_of y to get y -> z. Step 2: Apply Transitivity to x->y and y->z to get x->z.

steps.title: Apply Reflexivity to z subset_of y
steps.apply: Since z is a subset of y, by the Reflexivity axiom we derive y -> z.
steps.note: This is a trivial FD — y determines any subset of itself.

steps.title: Apply Transitivity
steps.formula_raw: x -> y and y -> z => x -> z (Transitivity)
steps.apply: We have x -> y (given) and y -> z (from step 1). By Transitivity, x -> z.
steps.note: This is the Decomposition rule: if X -> Y and Z is a subset of Y, then X -> Z.

solution.result: x -> z is derived by Reflexivity (z subset y gives y->z) followed by Transitivity with x->y.

questions.formula_note: Decomposition derived rule: X->Y and Z subset Y implies X->Z; proven via Reflexivity then Transitivity from Armstrong's axioms.

questions.question:
Match the pairs in the following questions:

Group 1:
(a) Secondary Index
(b) Non-procedural query language
(c) Closure of a set of attributes
(d) Natural join

Group 2:
(p) Function dependency
(q) B-tree
(r) Domain calculus
(s) Relational algebraic operations

A. a-q, b-r, c-p, d-s
B. a-p, b-r, c-q, d-s
C. a-q, b-p, c-r, d-s
D. a-r, b-q, c-p, d-s
questions.to_find: Match each database concept in Group 1 with its related concept in Group 2.

understand.plain: This question tests knowledge of core database concepts and which data structure or formalism they are most closely associated with. Secondary indexes use B-trees for efficient retrieval; domain calculus is a non-procedural query language; closure of attributes is computed using functional dependencies; and natural join is a relational algebraic operation.

keywords.explain: An index on a non-primary key attribute; typically implemented using a B-tree for efficient range queries.
keywords.example: An index on 'Salary' in an Employee table is a secondary index.

keywords.explain: A non-procedural query language based on logical predicates over domain variables; equivalent in power to relational algebra.
keywords.example: { <x,y> | Employee(x,y) AND y='CS' } is a domain calculus expression.

keywords.term: Closure of a set of attributes
keywords.explain: The set of all attributes that can be derived from a given set using functional dependencies; used to find candidate keys.
keywords.example: If A->B and B->C, then the closure of {A} is {A,B,C}.

keywords.explain: A relational algebraic operation that joins two relations on all common attributes.
keywords.example: R(A,B) natural join S(B,C) = R join S on R.B = S.B, projecting out duplicate B.

understand.visual_alt: Matching diagram: Secondary Index <-> B-tree; Non-procedural query language <-> Domain calculus; Closure of attributes <-> Functional dependency; Natural join <-> Relational algebraic operations.

given.aim: Match each concept in Group 1 to its related concept in Group 2.

terms.meaning: Secondary indexes are most commonly implemented using B-tree data structures for efficient ordered retrieval.
terms.example: MySQL InnoDB secondary indexes are B-tree based.
terms.connects: B-tree supports O(log n) search, insert, delete for index operations.

terms.meaning: Domain relational calculus is a non-procedural (declarative) query language that specifies what to retrieve, not how.
terms.example: SQL is inspired by relational calculus; domain calculus uses domain variables.
terms.connects: Tuple calculus and domain calculus are both non-procedural; domain calculus = option r.

terms.term: (c) Closure of a set of attributes — (p) Function dependency
terms.meaning: Attribute closure is computed using functional dependencies; it is the central concept in FD-based reasoning.
terms.example: To find keys, compute closures of attribute sets using given FDs.
terms.connects: Without FDs, closure computation is meaningless.

terms.meaning: Natural join is one of the fundamental relational algebraic operations.
terms.example: R |><| S (natural join) is a derived operation in relational algebra.
terms.connects: Natural join is defined in terms of Cartesian product + selection + projection.

given.plan: For each concept in Group 1, identify which concept in Group 2 it is most associated with by domain knowledge, then select the matching option.

steps.title: Match Secondary Index
steps.apply: Secondary Index is a database index structure. B-trees (q) are the standard data structure used for secondary indexes in databases.

steps.title: Match Non-procedural query language
steps.apply: A non-procedural query language specifies what data to retrieve without specifying how. Domain calculus (r) is a non-procedural formal query language.

steps.title: Match Closure of a set of attributes
steps.apply: Closure of a set of attributes is computed using functional dependencies (p). Given FDs, we compute which attributes are determined by a given set.

steps.title: Match Natural Join
steps.apply: Natural join (d) is one of the core relational algebraic operations (s). It joins two relations on their common attributes.
steps.note: d <-> s. Final matching: a-q, b-r, c-p, d-s => Option A.

questions.formula_note: Conceptual matching of database structures and formalisms: B-tree for indexing, domain calculus as non-procedural query language, attribute closure via FDs, natural join as relational algebra operation.

questions.question:
Indicate which of the following statements are true:
A relational database which is in 3NF may still have undesirable data redundancy because there may exist:
A. Transitive functional dependencies
B. Non-trivial functional dependencies involving prime attributes on the right-side.
C. Non-trivial functional dependencies involving prime attributes only on the left-side.
D. Non-trivial functional dependencies involving only prime attributes.
questions.answer: B. Non-trivial functional dependencies involving prime attributes on the right-side.
questions.to_find: Identify which type of FD can cause residual data redundancy in a 3NF relation.

understand.plain: Third Normal Form (3NF) eliminates transitive dependencies on non-prime attributes, but it does NOT eliminate all redundancy. A relation in 3NF can still have redundancy if there are non-trivial FDs where the right-hand side is a prime attribute (an attribute that is part of some candidate key). Boyce-Codd Normal Form (BCNF) is stricter and handles these cases.

keywords.explain: A relation is in 3NF if for every non-trivial FD X->A, either X is a superkey or A is a prime attribute.
keywords.example: R(A,B,C) with A->B, B->C: if {A,C} is the only key, then B->C violates 3NF (B not superkey, C not prime). With two keys {A,B} and {A,C}, it might be in 3NF.

keywords.explain: An attribute that is part of at least one candidate key.
keywords.example: In R(A,B,C) with candidate keys {A,B} and {B,C}: A, B, C are all prime.

keywords.explain: Stricter than 3NF: for every non-trivial FD X->A, X must be a superkey. Eliminates all FD-based redundancy.
keywords.example: A 3NF relation that is not in BCNF has FDs X->A where X is not a superkey even if A is prime.

understand.visual_alt: 3NF allows FDs of the form X->A where A is a prime attribute and X is not a superkey. These can cause redundancy not eliminated by 3NF but eliminated by BCNF.

given.aim: Identify which type of functional dependency causes residual redundancy in a 3NF but not BCNF relation.

terms.meaning: For every non-trivial FD X->A in R: X is a superkey OR A is a prime attribute.
terms.example: R(A,B,C) with FDs A->B, BC->A; keys {A} and {BC}: A->B has A as superkey (ok). BC->A has A as prime (ok). So R is in 3NF.
terms.connects: The second condition (A is prime) is the loophole that allows redundancy.

terms.meaning: Transitive dependencies on non-prime attributes cause 2NF/3NF violations, but 3NF specifically eliminates transitive dependencies on non-prime attributes.
terms.example: A->B->C where A is key and C is non-prime violates 3NF; already eliminated.
terms.connects: Eliminated by 3NF — not the cause of residual redundancy in 3NF relations.

terms.term: Option B: Non-trivial FDs with prime attribute on right-side
terms.meaning: 3NF permits FDs X->A where X is not a superkey but A is a prime attribute. These can still cause data redundancy.
terms.example: In a relation with two overlapping keys, an FD from one key to a partial component of another key is allowed by 3NF but causes redundancy.
terms.connects: This is the correct answer — 3NF does not address these FDs.

given.plan: Review the 3NF definition and identify which type of FD is still permitted. 3NF only prohibits non-prime attributes on the right side of non-superkey FDs; prime attributes on the right side are allowed, causing residual redundancy.

steps.title: Recall 3NF definition
steps.formula_raw: For every non-trivial FD X -> A: X is a superkey OR A is a prime attribute
steps.apply: 3NF eliminates transitive dependencies where non-prime attributes depend on non-superkey attributes. Option A (transitive FDs on non-prime attributes) is already eliminated by 3NF.
steps.note: So Option A cannot be the source of residual redundancy in 3NF.

steps.title: Identify permitted FDs in 3NF that cause redundancy
steps.formula_raw: X -> A is allowed in 3NF if A is prime (even if X is not a superkey)
steps.apply: When A is a prime attribute and X is not a superkey, 3NF still permits X -> A. Such FDs can create redundancy. This matches Option B: non-trivial FDs involving prime attributes on the right-side.
steps.note: BCNF would reject such FDs (BCNF requires X to be a superkey always), which is why BCNF is strictly stronger than 3NF.

steps.title: Evaluate remaining options
steps.apply: Option C (prime attributes only on left-side) and Option D (only prime attributes) do not specifically capture the 3NF loophole. The key issue is prime attributes on the RIGHT side of an FD from a non-superkey.
steps.note: Option B is the precise and correct answer.

solution.result: B. Non-trivial functional dependencies involving prime attributes on the right-side cause residual data redundancy in 3NF relations.

questions.formula_note: 3NF vs BCNF: 3NF permits X->A when A is prime (even if X is not a superkey), leading to residual redundancy; BCNF eliminates this by requiring X to always be a superkey.
